retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Mylohyoid Muscle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The mylohyoid muscle mainly obtains its blood supply from the mylohyoid artery, a branch of the inferior alveolar artery, which is a branch of the first portion of the maxillary artery. The maxillary artery supplies components within the face and is a tributary of the external carotid artery formed deep to the mandible neck. A common course of the maxillary artery is from its origin point deep to the mandible neck extending between the mandible ramus and ligament of the sphenomandibular to the lateral pterygoid muscle and finally to the fossa of the pterygopalatine. Hence, one frequently sees references to the maxillary artery as having three divisions, which include the pterygoid, pterygopalatine, and mandibular segments. The mandibular portion (bony portion) has five major branches including the middle meningeal artery, accessory meningeal artery, deep auricular artery, anterior tympanic artery, and the inferior alveolar artery. The inferior alveolar artery yields the mylohyoid branch just before entering the mandibular foramen. The mylohyoid branch runs in the mylohyoid groove where it functions as the primary vascular source for the mylohyoid muscle. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Hyoid Bone -- Surgical Considerations -- The Hyoid and Its Relation to Blood Vessels
retrieved_docs.content: The tip of the greater horn of the hyoid serves as a key landmark for cervical surgery in the midneck region. The carotid bifurcation and the superior thyroid and lingual arteries are closely related to the tip of the greater horn. The tip of the greater horn is a landmark for locating these arteries during cervical surgery. The carotid bifurcation lies inferior and posterior to the tip of the greater horn. The origin of the superior thyroid artery, the 1st branch of the external carotid artery, lies below the level of the greater horn. The lingual artery branches off the external carotid artery above the level of the greater horn.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Hyoid Bone -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: Arterial blood supply to the hyoid bone primarily originates from branches of the external carotid artery. The lingual artery, a major branch, gives rise to the suprahyoid branch, which courses along the superior border of the hyoid, supplying both the bone and the associated musculature. The submental branch of the facial artery also contributes to vascularization of the suprahyoid region, supporting the overlying muscles. Inferiorly, the superior thyroid artery provides the infrahyoid branch, which travels beneath the thyrohyoid muscle and along the lower border of the hyoid, further reinforcing the vascular network in this area. [4] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Maxillary Arteries -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: There are many physiologic variants within the maxillary artery and its course along with lateral pterygoid muscle, relative to whether it travels lateral to the lateral pterygoid or medial to the lateral pterygoid. Some studies have shown that there is a 66% chance it travels laterally and 33% that it travels medially. Other variants include a deep auricular and anterior tympanic artery branching from a common trunk versus individually coming off the maxillary artery. Another variation consists of the middle meningeal artery and accessory meningeal artery coming from a common trunk versus independently branching off the maxillary artery. Interestingly enough one study has found that cadavers with a medially coursing maxillary artery generally have the middle meningeal and accessory meningeal coming off the maxillary as separate arteries while a laterally coursing maxillary artery has shown the middle and accessory meningeal arteries coming off as a common trunk. Another interesting finding was while the majority of people have mirror tracks of the maxillary artery, a minimal number of patients could have one side of the maxillary artery traveling laterally to the muscle and medially on the contralateral side. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Medial (Internal) Pterygoid Nerve -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary artery supplies the medial pterygoid muscle through its pterygoid branches and by the facial artery through its muscular branches. The tensor veli palatini receives supply from the ascending palatine and accessory meningeal arteries . The tensor tympani muscle receives its arterial supply from the superior tympanic artery, which is a branch of the middle meningeal artery. All three of these muscles receive their innervation from the medial pterygoid nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Ascending Palatine Artery -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- Blood Supply
retrieved_docs.content: The blood supply begins in the external carotid artery from which the facial artery branches. The facial artery then gives rise to the ascending palatine artery which then anastomoses with the tonsillar artery, ascending pharyngeal artery, and the descending palatine artery. The blood then continues to supply the soft palate; palatoglossus, palatopharyngeus, tensor veli palatini, musculus uvulae, and the levator veli palatine, as well as the eustachian tube, and palatine tonsils. [1] [2] The prestyloid compartment, also known as the parapharyngeal space, receives the majority of its blood supply from the ascending palatine artery. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Hyoglossus Muscle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The hyoglossus muscle receives a dual blood supply. The main supply originates from the lingual artery, which is the third branch of the external carotid artery. The lingual artery gives rise to four main branches: The deep lingual artery The sublingual artery The suprahyoid branch The dorsal lingual branch The sublingual artery branches off the lingual artery at the anterior border of the hyoglossus muscle and is the branch of the lingual artery that supplies the hyoglossus.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Anterior: Common Carotid Arteries -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: While inferior to the hyoid bone, there is another group of four paired muscles called the infrahyoid muscles: sternothyroid, sternohyoid, thyrohyoid, and omohyoid. The major function of these muscles depresses the hyoid bone and elevates the larynx to facilitate swallowing. While all the infrahyoid muscles depress the hyoid bone, the sternohyoid muscles also depress the larynx due to their shared origin from the anterior surface of the sternoclavicular joint to attach to the body of the hyoid bone. This is in contrast with the thyrohyoid muscle that elevates the larynx because it starts at the thyroid cartilage and attaches to the greater horn of the hyoid bone. The omohyoid muscle is unique because it has two bellies similar to the digastric muscle. This muscle starts from the superior border of the scapula and attaches to the body of the hyoid bone. Along with the attachment to the hyoid bone, the omohyoid muscle also serves a minor role in the protection of the carotid sheath's contents.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Muscles -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary artery typically divides into 3 parts. The 1st is the mandibular part, with branches passing through the mandibular foramen, including the inferior alveolar, middle meningeal, accessory meningeal, deep auricular, and anterior tympanic arteries. The inferior alveolar artery supplies the mylohyoid muscle. The 2nd part branches into the anterior and posterior deep temporal branches, which supply the temporalis. The 2nd part also sends branches to the pterygoid, buccinator, and masseter. The 3rd part branches into the infraorbital, sphenopalatine, posterior superior alveolar, descending palatine, pharyngeal, and pterygoid canal arteries. The 3rd part's pterygopalatine branches supply the pterygopalatine fossa. The 3rd part's branches are not critical to facial musculature perfusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lingual Artery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The lingual artery, which supplies the tongue as well as the oral floor, is a major branch of the external carotid artery. It appears anteromedially from the external carotid artery, at the tip of the greater horn of the hyoid bone, between the superior thyroid artery and the facial artery. [1] The lingual artery moves medially to the hyoid bone while crossing the hypoglossal nerve (CN XII). It then courses deep to the stylohyoid and digastric muscles while subsequently passing between the middle constrictor and the hyoglossus muscles. [2] As the lingual artery progresses to the tip of the tongue, it branches to supply the adjacent tissues. The major branches of the lingual artery include the suprahyoid artery, the dorsal lingual artery, the sublingual artery, and the deep lingual artery, also known as the ranine artery. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Facial Muscles -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The facial muscles are predominantly perfused by the external carotid artery, via the facial artery, and the internal carotid artery. The facial artery is the external carotid artery's 4th branch after the superior thyroid, ascending pharyngeal, and lingual arteries. The facial artery is inferior to the occipital, posterior auricular, superficial temporal, and internal maxillary branches. The facial artery's 1st major branch, the submental artery, supplies the anterior digastric, a portion of the platysma, and the mylohyoid. The inferior alveolar artery, which runs through the mandible, also supplies the mylohyoid. The occipital artery perfuses the posterior digastric muscle, while the suprascapular artery supplies the remainder of the platysma. The facial artery crosses the gonial notch and transits toward the oral commissure, where it feeds the perioral muscles by branching into the inferior labial and superior labial arteries. The superior branch becomes the angular artery at the nasofacial junction. [14] [15] [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Anterior: Common Carotid Arteries -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The external carotid arteries have eight major divisions that supply the head, neck, and face. As the external carotid arteries ascend the neck, it gives off a branch called the superior thyroid artery. The superior thyroid artery is vital for the muscles, organs, and structures predominantly inferior to the hyoid bone like the thyroid gland, infrahyoid muscles, and sternocleidomastoid muscle, but also the larynx above the hyoid bone. The second branch from the external carotid arteries is the ascending pharyngeal arteries. These arteries supply the middle ear, pharynx part of the oral cavity, prevertebral muscles and ascend to supply the meninges. The next branch that arises is the lingual artery that supplies the floor of the mouth and tongue. As the external carotid artery approach the mandible, it gives off the facial artery. The facial artery has multiple regions of the face, palate part of the oral cavity and tonsils, and submandibular glands that it supplies. The external carotid supplies the scalp through the next branch, which is the occipital artery. The posterior auricular artery is the last branch before the external carotid artery terminates into end branches. This artery is important in supplying the ear, scalp, cranial never VII, and parts of the scalp. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Maxillary Arteries -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Mandibular (1 part) of the maxillary artery [3] : Deep auricular artery : enters the squamotympanic fissure Anterior tympanic artery : enters the squamotympanic fissure Middle meningeal artery : courses between the tensor veli palatine muscle and lateral pterygoid muscle to enter the foramen spinosum into the middle cranial cavity Contains an anterior branch which travels vertex and crosses the pterion. Contains a posterior branch which travels posterior and superior to reach the middle cranial fossa Accessory meningeal artery : enters the foramen ovale Inferior alveolar artery : enters the mandibular foramen Gives rise to the mylohyoid artery (branch of the inferior alveolar artery before it enters the mandible) Mental artery (terminal branch of the inferior alveolar artery) Pterygoid (2 part) of the maxillary artery: Anterior, middle and posterior deep temporal arteries Buccal artery Masseteric artery Pterygoid branches Pterygopalatine (3 part) of the maxillary artery [4] :

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Internal Maxillary Arteries -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics -- The first division of the maxillary artery:
retrieved_docs.content: Deep auricular and anterior tympanic artery supplies the external acoustic meatus and deep surface of the tympanic membrane, respectively. The middle meningeal artery is a major branch of the internal maxillary artery and has very significant clinical scenarios discussed later. This artery mainly supplies the dura, bone, and cranial cavity of the middle cranial fossa. It is attached to the periosteal or outer layer of the dura. [5] The accessory meningeal artery also supplies similar structures. The ascending pharyngeal and occipital artery (branches of the external carotid) also give rise to posterior meningeal arteries supplying the dura within the posterior cranial fossa. The posterior meningeal artery coming from the ascending pharyngeal artery travels through the hypoglossal canal to reach the posterior cranial fossa. The inferior alveolar artery supplies all the lower teeth, lower lip, and chin (through the mental artery). The mylohyoid artery supplies the mylohyoid. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Suprahyoid Muscle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: All suprahyoid muscles receive vascular supply from branches of the external carotid artery. The anterior belly of the digastric muscle is supplied by the submental branch of the facial artery, while the posterior belly receives blood from the occipital and posterior auricular arteries. Branches of the lingual artery supply the geniohyoid muscle. The mylohyoid muscle receives blood from the submental branch of the facial artery and the mylohyoid branch of the inferior alveolar artery. [18] Lymph from the suprahyoid region flows into the submental and submandibular lymph nodes before ultimately draining into the deep cervical lymph nodes.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Thyrohyoid Membrane -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The superior and inferior laryngeal arteries supply most of the blood to the larynx.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1694
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as تروية دموية and is a subcategory of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck, indicating that interventions directed at blood vessels are considered vascularization procedures and belong to the broader group of head and neck vessel procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: situates these processes in the basilar artery (Arteria basilaris) and the broader Structure of intracranial artery, which serves as a finding site for occlusion/stenosis, transient cerebral ischemia, and intracranial atherosclerosis, and as an indirect procedure site for endarterectomy of head and neck vessels (endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck) under the broader procedure category endarterectomy (استئصال باطنة الشريان). This integrated network supports comprehensive assessment and management of precerebral arterial disease, its neurologic manifestations, and systemic consequences.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 891
retrieved_docs.content: The structure of intracranial artery includes the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component. This intracranial arterial structure is a recognized site for the finding of تصلب عصيدي في المخ (intracranial atherosclerosis). Although direct surgical access to intracranial arteries is limited, endarterectomy of vessels of the head and neck is considered an indirect procedural approach that may affect the structure of intracranial artery. Understanding the relationships among the basilar artery, intracranial atherosclerotic disease, and head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is important for comprehensive cerebrovascular assessment and treatment planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 2587
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is a medical intervention that targets vascular structures and is categorized under the broader group of other procedures on vessels of the head and neck. Within this category, specific activities such as hemodynamic monitoring and vascular cannula insertion are recognized as distinct but related procedures. This hierarchical organization highlights that procedures on blood vessels encompass both monitoring of circulatory dynamics and the placement of vascular cannulas, and they are considered a subset of the larger collection of head and neck vascular procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2244
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as a type of تروية دموية (blood perfusion) and is positioned as a subcategory within other or procedures on vessels of head and neck. In addition, Procedure on blood vessel is linked to hemodynamické monitorování (hemodynamic monitoring) through an inverse‑is‑a relationship, indicating that the procedure is encompassed by the broader concept of hemodynamic monitoring. This hierarchical and relational structure clarifies how vascular procedures are organized within head and neck interventions and how they relate to monitoring of blood flow dynamics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 231
retrieved_docs.content: surgical removal of atherosclerotic plaque—endarterectomy of head‑and‑neck vessels (استئصال باطنة الشريان)—is indicated in the context of arterial thrombosis or embolism. The intracranial arterial structure can reveal intracerebral atherosclerotic changes, providing an indirect site for such procedures. Together, these entities illustrate the interconnected pathophysiology of precerebral arterial disease, basilar artery involvement, cerebral infarction, transient ischemic events, clinical presentation, and both medical and surgical management strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 1984
retrieved_docs.content: Procedure on blood vessel is classified as a type of تروية دموية (blood perfusion) and is placed under the broader category of other or procedures on vessels of head and neck. The anatomical location referred to as cévy is identified as a procedure_site_of for تروية دموية, indicating that interventions described as Procedure on blood vessel are performed at the cévy site. This hierarchy connects the specific vascular procedure to its anatomical context and to the general group of head‑and‑neck vascular interventions, facilitating organized clinical documentation and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 1026
retrieved_docs.content: The Structure of intracranial artery encompasses the arteria basilaris as a subordinate component, indicating that the basilar artery is a child element within the overall architecture of intracranial arterial structures. This anatomical framework serves as a common site for the finding described as تصلب عصيدي في المخ, reflecting the presence of intracranial atherosclerotic disease within these vessels. Surgical management of vascular pathology in the head and neck region often involves an endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck, a procedure that is indirectly related to the Structure of intracranial artery because interventions on extracranial vessels can affect or be associated with intracranial arterial anatomy. The endarterectomy; vessel of head and neck itself is classified under the broader procedural category استئصال باطنة الشريان (arterial endarterectomy), establishing a hierarchical relationship in which the specific head‑and‑neck endarterectomy is a subset of general arterial endarterectomy. Understanding these anatomical, pathological, and procedural connections is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and the planning of appropriate surgical or endovascular treatments involving intracranial and extracranial arteries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 162
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified is a broad hierarchical category that encompasses a variety of specialized medical procedure groups. Within this category, Adjunct Vascular System Procedures is classified not only as a child of the general “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified” but also as a sub‑category of other non‑or therapeutic cardiovascular procedures and of other procedures on vessels other than head and neck, indicating its relevance to vascular interventions that are neither purely therapeutic nor limited to the head and neck region. Computer assisted surgery [CAS] is likewise a child of Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified and is linked to several imaging‑related parent categories, including other diagnostic radiology and related techniques, magnetic resonance imaging, and other CT scan, reflecting its reliance on advanced radiologic guidance. In addition, CAS has a specific child, Computer assisted surgery with
